ottoman_empire.name: Ottoman Empire
ottoman_empire.summary:
In 1453 AD, Ottoman Sultan Mehmed II achieved something that had been attempted and failed for over a thousand years: the conquest of Constantinople. The fall of the last Roman capital sent shockwaves across Christendom and ended the Byzantine Empire after 1,100 years. By 1500, the Ottomans control the Balkans, Anatolia, and are expanding into the Middle East and North Africa.

Under Sultan Bayezid II, the Ottoman Empire is consolidating its enormous gains. The empire is a sophisticated multi-ethnic, multi-religious state where Christians, Jews, and Muslims live under Ottoman rule. The devshirme system — recruiting Christian boys to be trained as elite Janissary soldiers and administrators — creates one of the most effective military-administrative machines of the era.
key_events: Fall of Constantinople (1453) ends the Byzantine Empire
Ottomans control the Silk Road and trade routes to Asia
Janissary corps — professional soldiers of unmatched discipline
Jewish refugees expelled from Spain (1492) welcomed into Ottoman territories
ottoman_empire.did_you_know: When Spain expelled its Jewish population in 1492, the same year Columbus sailed, Sultan Bayezid II actively welcomed the refugees, reportedly saying that Ferdinand of Spain was 'impoverishing his own country to enrich mine.' Ottoman Jewry flourished for centuries as a result.

ming_chinese_empire.name: Ming Dynasty
ming_chinese_empire.summary:
The Ming Dynasty governs China from the Forbidden City in Beijing, the greatest palace complex ever built — over 9,000 rooms behind walls painted blood red. Founded after overthrowing the Mongol Yuan dynasty in 1368, the Ming represent a restoration of Han Chinese rule and Confucian values.

Just decades earlier, Admiral Zheng He commanded the largest wooden ships ever built in history — treasure fleets of over 300 vessels reaching East Africa. But in 1433, China made a fateful decision: the voyages were cancelled, the ships burned, and maritime trade was restricted. As the Portuguese were beginning their exploration of Africa, China was turning inward — a decision that would have enormous consequences for the next five centuries.
key_events: Forbidden City completed (1420) — the world's largest palace complex
Zheng He's treasure fleets reach East Africa (1405–1433), then cancelled
Great Wall rebuilt and extended in stone — the version that survives today
Ming porcelain traded across the world — 'china' becomes synonymous with ceramics
ming_chinese_empire.did_you_know: Zheng He's treasure ships were truly colossal — the largest were roughly 400 feet long, compared to Columbus's Santa Maria at 85 feet. China had the capability to reach the Americas before Columbus. The decision not to continue exploration is one of history's great what-ifs.

holy_roman_empire.name: Habsburg Empire
holy_roman_empire.summary:
The Habsburg family, through strategic marriages rather than conquest, have assembled the largest realm in Europe by 1500. 'Others may wage war; you, happy Austria, marry!' — the phrase captures how the Habsburgs expanded their empire. The young Charles of Habsburg will soon inherit Spain (with its new American empire), the Netherlands, and the Holy Roman Empire simultaneously, making him the most powerful ruler in the Western world.

This is the age when Spain, under Ferdinand and Isabella, has just concluded the Reconquista, expelled Jews and Muslims, and sent Columbus across the Atlantic. The Americas are about to dramatically transform Europe's economy and global power.
key_events: Columbus reaches the Americas (1492) — the Habsburg-Spanish connection soon controls this
Reconquista completed — Spain expels Moors and Jews (1492)
Martin Luther will soon post his 95 Theses (1517) — triggering the Protestant Reformation
Habsburg marriages create the largest European empire through dynastic union
holy_roman_empire.did_you_know: At his peak, Charles V (Habsburg) ruled over Spain, the Americas, Austria, the Netherlands, southern Italy, and was Holy Roman Emperor — an empire on which 'the sun never set' a century before Britain claimed that phrase. He ruled over roughly a quarter of the world's population.

aztec_empire.name: Aztec Empire
aztec_empire.summary:
Tenochtitlan, the Aztec capital built on an island in Lake Texcoco, is one of the largest cities in the world in 1500 — larger than any contemporary European city. Its population of perhaps 200,000–300,000 people is served by aqueducts, causeways, floating gardens (chinampas), and a sophisticated market system. When Spanish soldiers first saw it in 1519, they compared it to Venice and could hardly believe their eyes.

The Aztec Triple Alliance controls central Mexico through a tributary empire, demanding goods and sacrificial victims from subjugated peoples. Human sacrifice on a massive scale — dedicated to keeping the sun rising — makes the Aztecs feared and hated by their neighbors, who will prove decisive allies to the Spanish conquistadors.
key_events: Tenochtitlan — one of the world's largest cities, built on a lake island
Aztec Triple Alliance controls central Mexico through tribute and military power
Massive pyramid temples and ritual human sacrifice at the heart of Aztec religion
Hernán Cortés will arrive in 1519 — the empire will fall by 1521
aztec_empire.did_you_know: The Aztec 'Flower Wars' were ritualized conflicts fought specifically to capture, not kill, enemy warriors for sacrifice. It was an elegant military-religious system — until they faced Spanish soldiers who had no interest in capturing anyone and were immune to the psychological impact of expected sacrifice.

inca_empire.name: Inca Empire
inca_empire.summary:
The Inca Empire — Tawantinsuyu, 'the four regions' — is the largest empire in pre-Columbian America, stretching 4,300 kilometers along the Pacific coast of South America. Without a written language or the wheel, the Incas managed an empire of perhaps 10 million people through an extraordinary administrative system based on knotted strings (quipus), a road network rivaling Rome's, and mit'a (labor tribute) from subject peoples.

At its center is the 'navel of the world' — Cusco, at 3,400 meters altitude, connected to the empire by stone roads and relay runners who could carry messages 240 kilometers a day. The Inca state controlled food storage in thousands of storehouses, redistributing resources to prevent famine. Francisco Pizarro and 168 soldiers will shatter this civilization in 1532.
key_events: Inca road system — 40,000 km of roads through extreme Andean terrain
Machu Picchu built — though abandoned before the Spanish arrival
Quipu system — knotted strings used for record-keeping without writing
Mit'a labor system redistributes work and goods across the empire
inca_empire.did_you_know: The Inca Empire was the largest empire in the pre-Columbian Americas — larger than the Roman Empire at its peak in absolute land area. And it was conquered by 168 Spanish soldiers, 62 horsemen, and a devastating epidemic of smallpox that had swept ahead of the Spanish, killing the Inca emperor Huayna Capac before Pizarro even arrived.

songhai.name: Songhai Empire
songhai.summary:
The Songhai Empire is the largest empire in African history at this period, controlling the gold and salt trade routes that were the economic arteries of West Africa. The city of Timbuktu — now synonymous with 'the ends of the earth' — is in 1500 a flourishing center of Islamic scholarship, with the Sankore Madrasah housing tens of thousands of manuscripts and thousands of students.

Askia Muhammad, who seizes power in 1493, rules with a sophistication that impresses even the Muslim scholars and travelers who visit. He standardizes weights and measures, promotes Islamic law, and maintains diplomatic relations with the Ottoman Empire and North African states.
key_events: Timbuktu — major center of Islamic learning with hundreds of thousands of manuscripts
Gold and salt trade makes Songhai enormously wealthy
Askia Muhammad standardizes weights, measures, and law across the empire
Songhai will be destroyed by Moroccan invasion in 1591
songhai.did_you_know: The libraries of Timbuktu hold 700,000–1,000,000 manuscripts on mathematics, astronomy, medicine, and philosophy. Many were hidden from French colonial authorities in the 19th and 20th centuries and are only now being digitized. Sub-Saharan Africa had a rich written tradition that colonial-era historians largely ignored.

mughal_empire.name: Mughal Empire
mughal_empire.summary:
In 1500, the Mughal Empire does not yet exist — but its founder Babur is already at work. The Timurid prince from Central Asia, descended from both Genghis Khan and Tamerlane, is fighting to hold Kabul while watching for his opportunity to invade India. In 1526, at the First Battle of Panipat, Babur's use of artillery and flanking cavalry will defeat the Delhi Sultanate and begin one of history's greatest empires.

The Indian subcontinent in 1500 is a patchwork of sultanates, Hindu kingdoms, and regional powers. The Delhi Sultanate, weakened by Tamerlane's devastating raid in 1398, no longer controls much of northern India. The stage is set for Mughal transformation.
mughal_empire.ruler: Ibrahim Lodi (Delhi Sultanate, r. 1517–1526 AD) — soon to be overthrown
key_events: Babur prepares to invade India from Kabul
First Battle of Panipat (1526) — Mughal Empire begins
Portuguese establish coastal trading posts in India (1498 onward)
Indian Ocean trade routes being disrupted by Portuguese naval power
mughal_empire.did_you_know: The Taj Mahal, the most iconic Mughal monument, was built 1632–1653 by Emperor Shah Jahan as a tomb for his wife Mumtaz Mahal. It required 22 years, 20,000 workers, and materials from across Asia. It sits on a subcontinent that accounts for roughly 25% of global GDP today — a proportion it also held during the height of the Mughal era.
